slides.title: What a property really is
body: A property looks like a variable from outside the class but secretly runs code when read or written.

Real example: Salesforce's standard Account.Name field — when you read or write it, validation, audit logging, and indexing all happen behind the scenes. It LOOKS like a simple field but it's a property with hidden machinery.

vocabulary.def: A class member that exposes data with optional get/set logic running behind the scenes.
The 'get' block of a property — runs when something reads the value.
The 'set' block of a property — runs when something writes a new value.

slides.title: Auto-properties — get; set;
body: When you don't need any custom logic, use the shorthand: { get; set; }. Apex auto-generates trivial getter and setter for you.
This is what 99% of properties look like in real Apex code. You only write custom getter/setter when you need validation, lazy loading, or computation.

slides.title: Auto-property
slides.intro: Type this. Same end-result as a public field, but the getter/setter shape lets you upgrade later without breaking callers.
lineExplanations: Auto-property: 'name' is a String. The { get; set; } says 'I'll let people read and write this with default behavior.'
Same shape for age. Closing } closes the class.

slides.title: Using the property
slides.intro: Type this. From the outside, properties read and write exactly like a field.
lineExplanations: Write to .name — the auto-generated setter stores the value.
Read .name — the auto-generated getter returns the stored value.

slides.title: Read-only properties
body: If you want callers to READ a value but not WRITE it, leave out 'set'. Or make set private to allow only internal class code to modify.
Real example: a Stripe Charge has an 'id' field that is read-only after creation. The constructor sets it; nothing else can. This guarantees ids stay immutable.

slides.title: Read-only id
slides.intro: Type this. Outsiders can read .id but only the class itself can set it.
lineExplanations: id has a public get and a PRIVATE set — only this class can assign it.
Inside the class, we can write to id (private set is reachable). 'this' refers to the current instance.

slides.title: Custom getter / setter
body: When you need validation or computation, write the get/set block bodies yourself.
Real example: a User.email property whose setter trims whitespace and lowercases the input. Or an Order.total property whose getter sums line items on the fly.

slides.title: Setter that validates
slides.intro: Ari's color-validation example. The class accepts only RED or WHITE. Any other value silently falls back to WHITE — the property's setter enforces the invariant so callers can't store bogus state.
lineExplanations: Static constant for the red option.
Static constant for the white option.
Private backing field — the actual storage. The property reads/writes this.
Public property named 'color' — looks like a field from outside, but has logic inside.
Getter — runs whenever someone READS car.color. Just return the backing field.
Setter — runs whenever someone ASSIGNS to car.color. The implicit 'value' is what they passed.
Validate: is the new value one of our acceptable options?
Default to white when an invalid value comes in. Silent fallback — no exception.
Try to assign 'green'. The setter rejects it and falls back to white.
Reading the property runs the getter. Prints 'white' even though we set 'green'.

slides.title: Computed (lazy) properties
body: A getter can compute its value on the fly — no backing field needed at all. Useful when the value is derived from other data.
Real example: an Order's totalAmount might compute from a list of line items each time it's read. No risk of the cache going stale.

slides.title: Computed property
slides.intro: Type this. fullName has no backing field — the getter computes from firstName and lastName.
lineExplanations: Custom getter computes from the two fields. No setter — fullName is purely derived.

slides.front: Why use a property instead of just a public field?
slides.back: Properties let you add validation, computation, or access control LATER without breaking the callers that read or write 'obj.field' syntax.
slides.detail: Public fields are a one-way door. Properties are a hinge — same call site, swappable backing logic.

slides.question: You want callers to read 'orderId' but only your class's constructor to set it. Which property declaration?
options: public String orderId { get; private set; }
slides.explanation: Public get + private set. Anyone can read it; only this class (including its constructor) can write it.

slides.title: Capstone: Order with computed total + normalized email
slides.intro: Build an Order class with two properties that do real work: customerEmail normalizes input on every set (trim + lowercase), and total computes itself from subtotal + taxRate on every read. The same getter/setter pattern Salesforce uses on its own validation-heavy fields.
lineExplanations: Backing field for the normalized email — private so callers can't bypass the property.
Public property with custom getter and setter.
Getter just returns the backing field.
If non-null, normalize: lowercase + strip whitespace.
Auto-property — Apex generates trivial get/set for simple values.
Computed read-only property — no backing field needed.
Getter computes on every read; never stale because the inputs are read fresh each time.
Construct an order to demo the properties.
Set the email — note the messy whitespace and uppercase. The setter will clean it.

slides.title: What you now know
body: Auto-properties, read-only properties, custom getter/setter for validation, and computed properties.
Next: SOQL — how Apex reads data from the Salesforce database. The most-used skill in any real Apex codebase.
